seo.page_title: Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line 2024 import to NZ
seo.meta_description: Import a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line from Japan to NZ with a 90.6 kWh battery, up to 590 km WLTP range and transparent landed-cost support.
seo.h1_tag: Import a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line to NZ
seo.og_title: Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line 2024 – Import to NZ
seo.og_description: Looking at a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line? We help NZ buyers source low‑km cars from Japan, with battery health reporting and clear landed costs.
seo.primary_keyword: Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line import NZ
secondary_keywords: Mercedes EQE AMG Line Japan import

blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line to NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: Bring a 2024 Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line in from Japan and get a 90.6 kWh premium EV sedan with up to 590 km WLTP range, set up for NZ commuting and weekend trips.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Kiwis look at the EQE 350 AMG Line for its 90.6 kWh battery, up to 590 km WLTP range and quiet motorway manners. It handles stop‑start city traffic smoothly, and rear‑wheel drive or 4MATIC options give confidence on wet roads and longer NZ runs.

savings_block.headline: What you could save vs NZ dealer pricing
savings_block.body: Recent 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line examples in Japan typically land in NZ from about $120,000 to $145,000 once you include auction price, shipping and on‑road costs. Local dealer sticker prices for similar cars are usually higher, especially for low‑km AMG Line trims. We map out the full landed estimate up front so you can compare it properly with NZ retail offers.

cta_form.headline: Find your EQE 350 AMG Line — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Here’s a typical 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line from Japan — note the AMG Line wheels, MBUX widescreen layout and how quietly it pulls away under electric power.
blocks.main_usp_deep: The 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line pairs a 90.6 kWh battery with a single motor rated at 288 hp and about 530 Nm, giving around 6.2 seconds 0–100 km/h without chewing through range. WLTP range is up to 590 km, which in NZ usually means comfortable return trips between most main centres with charging to spare. Adaptive cruise control, automatic emergency braking and a surround view camera help on busy motorways and tight carparks. MBUX infotainment and premium audio keep day‑to‑day driving easy enough to live with.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You’re likely a tech‑minded professional who wants a premium EV that still feels like a traditional luxury sedan inside. You care about real‑world range, battery health reports and how the car will handle NZ trips rather than just brochure numbers. If you like your gadgets but still want a calm, quiet drive, the EQE 350 AMG Line is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing cars from Japan since 2009, with more than 1,000 vehicles sourced for local buyers. You get full auction documentation, photos and our commentary before you decide to bid. We explain the landed cost in plain language, including shipping and NZ entry work, so there are fewer surprises. For EVs, we put extra focus on battery information and how the car will work in real NZ conditions.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Gives enough usable range for most NZ commutes and regular intercity trips without constant top‑ups.
Even allowing for NZ roads and weather, most owners can cover a week of city driving between charges.
Delivers smooth, quiet acceleration for motorway on‑ramps and passing slower traffic with ease.
Quick enough to feel responsive without sacrificing comfort or range on longer drives.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Adaptive cruise, AEB, surround view camera, MBUX infotainment
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Helps with long SH1 runs, tight city parking and keeping navigation, audio and vehicle settings simple to use.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how different EQE 350 AMG Line options from Japan typically compare for NZ buyers.

rows.notes: The EQE 350 AMG Line starts from 2024 in this spec, so all examples meet current ESC and safety feature expectations. Being new also helps with battery warranty coverage, depending on the specific car and market origin.

All EQE 350 AMG Line models use a 90.6 kWh battery and a single motor rated at 288 hp, with rear‑wheel drive or 4MATIC all‑wheel drive. RWD usually offers slightly better range, while 4MATIC adds traction for wet or unsealed roads.

AMG Line brings sportier exterior styling, AMG wheels and upgraded interior trim over standard EQE models. Some cars add options like premium audio, upgraded driver assistance packs or different wheel sizes that can affect ride comfort and range.

As a late‑model EV, the EQE 350 AMG Line typically meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. We check for any structural repairs, lighting changes or aftermarket parts that could slow entry certification.

Being a newer premium EV, numbers in Japan are growing but still limited compared with mass‑market models. That can mean waiting a little longer for the right colour, mileage and option mix, but it also helps keep quality higher.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For an EV like the EQE 350 AMG Line, we look for battery health information and note any software or driver‑assist comments on the auction sheet.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common models, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a premium EV is straightforward, but it helps to understand the key moving parts.

items.risk: You’re unsure how much battery degradation there is and what that means for range in a few years.
items.solution: We request battery data where available, look at age and mileage, and explain what that typically means for real‑world range in NZ. You see the information before you decide to bid.

items.risk: You’re worried about hidden costs on top of the auction price.
items.solution: We outline auction price, our fee, shipping, insurance, NZ entry work and on‑road costs separately so you can see how the total landed figure is built up.

items.risk: Auction grades and Japanese notes can be hard to interpret.
items.solution: We translate and explain the auction sheet, highlight any repairs or comments, and share photos so you can judge condition with more confidence.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: Tell us the year, budget and features you want in an EQE 350 AMG Line and we’ll shortlist real cars from the Japanese auctions. You choose which ones to pursue, and we handle bidding, shipping and NZ entry work. If you’d like to compare it with local options, we can run those numbers too.

faq_block.q: What real‑world range can I expect from a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line in NZ?
faq_block.a: With a 90.6 kWh battery and up to 590 km WLTP range, many NZ drivers see less than that in practice. In mixed city and motorway use, planning around 400–500 km between charges is a sensible starting point, depending on speed, weather and how you drive.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line from Japan?
faq_block.a: Recent examples usually land between about $120,000 and $145,000, depending on mileage, options and auction price. On top of the hammer price you’ll have our fee, shipping and insurance, NZ entry work, registration and on‑road costs.

faq_block.q: Will a Japanese‑import EQE 350 AMG Line meet NZ entry standards?
faq_block.a: As a new premium EV, this model typically meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final entry certification is done in NZ, so we can’t confirm the outcome until the car is inspected here.

faq_block.q: How long does the whole import process take?
faq_block.a: Once we win an auction, shipping to NZ is usually 2–4 weeks depending on the vessel schedule. Allow extra time to find the right car, clear it through the port and complete NZ entry certification before you can drive it home.

faq_block.q: How do auction grades relate to the condition of an EQE 350 AMG Line?
faq_block.a: Auction grades give a quick view of exterior, interior and overall condition, but the detail sits in the written notes and photos. We translate and explain these, point out any repairs or wear, and only recommend cars where the documentation lines up with what you’re expecting.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get occasional updates on EQE listings, EV import tips and recent landed examples in NZ.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based import service that’s been sourcing vehicles from Japan since 2009. We focus on matching individual buyers with specific cars rather than pushing in‑stock units. For EVs, we pay particular attention to battery information, charging hardware and how the car will work on NZ roads.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost is the total to get a 2024 EQE 350 AMG Line from a Japanese auction to registered on NZ plates.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay for the car in Japan, plus local auction and handling fees.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and marine insurance while it’s on the water.
Port charges, cleaning if required, NZ entry certification, registration and on‑road costs.

auction_checklist.check: Battery health report and charging history
auction_checklist.why: Gives a clearer picture of current and future range, and whether the car has been fast‑charged heavily.
MBUX and driver‑assist systems are central to how the EQE is used day to day, so glitches can be frustrating and costly to sort.
Curb rash or panel repairs can affect both appearance and resale; AMG wheels are more expensive to refurbish.
Confirm adaptive cruise, automatic emergency braking and cameras work as intended, as repairs can be complex.
Replacing missing Type 2 or CCS leads and adapters in NZ can add unexpected cost.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You want a low‑cost commuter EV and don’t need a large premium sedan.
You’re uncomfortable with any battery degradation risk and would only consider a brand‑new NZ‑new car with full local warranty.
You prefer a simpler interface and fewer driver‑assist systems than the MBUX‑heavy EQE offers.
You rarely drive beyond short city trips and might be better served by a smaller EV that’s easier to park.

alternatives_block.intro: If you’re still weighing things up, a few other imports can be worth a look.

rows.better_if: You want a slightly sportier drive feel and prefer BMW’s iDrive system layout.
rows.tradeoff: Range is typically a bit lower than the EQE and you miss out on the AMG Line styling cues.

rows.better_if: You prioritise maximum range and access to the Supercharger network across NZ.
rows.tradeoff: The cabin is more minimalist and some buyers miss the traditional luxury feel of a Mercedes interior.

rows.better_if: You want sharper handling and a more aggressive exterior design.
rows.tradeoff: Rear seat space is tighter and pricing is usually higher than a comparable EQE 350 AMG Line.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health on the EQE 350 AMG Line
battery_health_block.body: The EQE’s 90.6 kWh pack is designed for long‑distance use, but how it’s been charged and driven still matters. When we shortlist cars, we look for battery data, age, mileage and any notes about charging behaviour so you’re not guessing about future range.
checklist: Confirm any available battery state‑of‑health figures and how they were measured.
Check vehicle age and mileage against typical EQE degradation patterns.
Look for comments about frequent DC fast charging or taxi/fleet use in the auction notes.
Confirm that high‑voltage system warnings or EV‑related fault codes are not mentioned.
Factor in remaining manufacturer battery warranty where applicable to the specific car.

charging_block.headline: Charging a Mercedes EQE 350 AMG Line in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: With a Type 2 / CCS Combo port, the EQE 350 AMG Line is straightforward to charge at home using a wallbox or suitable socket. Most owners top up overnight, which is usually enough to cover several days of commuting given the 90.6 kWh battery and typical NZ driving patterns.
charging_block.public_charging: On the road, the CCS Combo connection works with most NZ fast‑charging networks. The EQE’s range means you can often skip chargers on shorter trips, but for longer runs we’ll help you think through likely stops based on where you live and drive.
caveats: Some older public chargers may have lower power output, so plan a bit more time on those stops.
Apartment or shared parking setups can need extra planning and body corporate approval for home charging.
Fast charging is handy, but relying on it heavily over time can affect long‑term battery health.

ev_running_costs.headline: Running costs for an EQE 350 AMG Line in NZ
ev_running_costs.body: With a 90.6 kWh battery and home charging, day‑to‑day costs are usually well below a similar non-EV luxury sedan. Using typical NZ power prices, a full charge at home often works out to a fraction of what you’d spend on a tank of running cost for the same distance, and servicing is mainly tyres, brakes and routine checks.
items: Home charging can be scheduled for off‑peak times to keep per‑km costs down.
Public fast charging is more expensive than home power but still often under comparable non-EV spend for the same trip.
Fewer moving parts in the electric powertrain can mean less routine maintenance than a combustion equivalent.
Tyres and brakes on a heavy, powerful EV still need quality replacements, so it’s worth budgeting for those.

range_real_world.headline: Real‑world range for NZ roads
range_real_world.body: On paper the EQE 350 AMG Line offers up to 590 km WLTP range, but NZ conditions are more varied than a lab test. We help you think about your actual routes — city, motorway and hills — and what that’s likely to mean for usable range day to day.
items: In flat cities like Christchurch or Hamilton, many drivers can comfortably cover a working week of commuting on a single charge.
In hillier areas like Wellington, stronger regenerative braking helps recover energy on descents but range can still dip with wind and steep climbs.
Regular Auckland–Hamilton or similar trips are usually manageable on one charge, with a buffer for detours.
Driving at higher motorway speeds, running big wheels or using climate control heavily will trim range, so we factor that into our guidance.
